purpose: GPU-only vs Hybrid (throughput-adaptive + cpu-first) on Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct, 500 prompts in/out 512.
host: H100 80GB x4 + Xeon Platinum 8480+ (96 vCPU KVM, 1 NUMA, 944 GB RAM)
router_stats_observed: Router stats [503 reqs]: GPU=58.9~59.5 tok/s (501 reqs), CPU=0.0 tok/s (2 reqs), cpu_ratio=0.4%, in_flight=1/1, adaptive_slots=1

key_findings.routing_behavior: throughput-adaptive accepted 2/503 reqs on CPU, then kept adaptive_slots=1 and routed remaining 501 reqs to GPU. CPU=0.0 tok/s observed (CPU req still in-flight at every stats interval).
key_findings.abort_slot_leak: not reproduced — 500/500 success, no stuck slot, hybrid wall ≈ gpu-only wall.
key_findings.performance: Hybrid throughput ≈ GPU-only throughput (within ±2%) because adaptive routing diverted nearly all traffic to GPU after probing CPU. Adding the CPU lane gave no measurable speedup at this batch shape (1.5B / in=out=512 / threads=8).
key_findings.cpu_threads_8_observation: user override threads=8 dramatically caps CPU's effective compute. Even on 96-core 8480+ with AMX, only 8 OMP workers run → 1.5B prefill 512 tokens stalls the CPU lane indefinitely while GPU finishes the rest of the burst.

implications.throughput_adaptive_correctness: EMA-based adaptive routing correctly detected the slow CPU lane and avoided sending more work to it. Capacity router's 'cpu-first' bias still let the first reqs probe CPU, then back-off.
implications.cpu_lane_value_at_1.5B: At 1.5B with this prompt shape and threads=8, the CPU lane has effectively 0 production value — it absorbs 0.4% of traffic and contributes 0 tok/s. The hybrid lane is a no-op safety net here.
next_actions: Bump HYBRID_CPU_THREADS toward 96 (or auto) and re-run to test whether 12x more threads pulls CPU above the threshold where adaptive routing keeps it engaged.
Try larger model (7B/70B) where GPU per-token cost is also higher — CPU lane may become productive relative to GPU's marginal cost.
Try int8/w8a8 quant + AMX_INT8 path (still pending overall).
